_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Syntax
summary: See how a small C++ program is put together and run.
refresher: A small C++ program usually includes a header, defines `main`, and places statements between braces. Most statements end with a semicolon. `std::cout` prints information on the screen.

blocks.body: C++ needs a little more structure than Python. The program below includes the tool used for printing, begins in a function named `main`, and places the work between `{` and `}`. Most action lines end with a semicolon.

Before C++ runs, a compiler translates the code into a program the computer can execute. If a semicolon, quote, or brace is missing, the compiler stops and points to a nearby line. Fix the first error, build again, and then look at anything that remains.

blocks.title: If you have seen Python
blocks.body: Python can run a short print statement by itself. C++ asks you to put that statement inside `main` and compile the program before it runs. Both languages still execute instructions from top to bottom.

blocks.caption: Read this from top to bottom: add the printing tool, begin main, print one line, and finish successfully.
A complete statement and balanced function body let compilation continue.

blocks.body: Read the corrected program one piece at a time. `main` is where execution starts. Braces surround the function's work. `std::cout` prints the text, and the semicolon finishes that instruction.

blocks.title: Start with the first error
blocks.body: One missing semicolon or brace can cause several compiler messages. Fix the earliest message first because the later ones may disappear with it.

blocks.prompt: Which line is a complete output statement?
blocks.why: Output uses the insertion operator and the statement ends with a semicolon.
A hosted C++ program begins by calling its main function.

blocks.title: Syntax checklist
items: Start with the first compiler error.
